channel.title: Calculus on Eric Windsor
channel.description: Recent content in Calculus on Eric Windsor

item.title: Multiple integration
item.description: Iterated Integrals and Area Iterated integration is the process of repeatedly integrating the results of previous integrations. Integrating one integral is denoted as follows. Let $a, b, c$ and $d$ be numbers and let $g_1 (x)$, $g_2 (x)$, $h_1 (y)$ and $h_2 (y)$ be functions of $x$ and $y$, respectively. Then: $$ \begin{align} \int_c^d \int_{h_1(y)}^{h_2(y)}f(x,y)dx dy = \int_c^d(\int_{h_1(y)}^{h_2(y)}f(x,y)dx)dy\\\\ \int_a^b \int_{g_1(y)}^{g_2(y)}f(x,y)dx dy = \int_a^b (\int_{g_1(y)}^{g_2(y)}f(x,y)dy) dx \end{align} $$ $x$ varies from $h_1(y)$ to $h_2(y)$, whereas $y$ varies from $c$ to $d$.

item.title: Vector
item.description: Defination of Vector A vector is a directed line segment. Given points $P$ and $Q$ (either in the plane or in space), we denote with $\vec {PQ}$ the vector from $P$ to $Q$. The point $P$ is said to be the initial point of the vector, and the point $Q$ is the terminal point. The magnitude, length or norm of $\vec {PQ}$ is the length of the line segment $\overline {PQ}$: $|| \overline{PQ} ||$ = $||\vec {PQ} ||$ .

item.title: Sequences
item.description: Sequence A sequence is a function $a(n)$ whose domain is $\mathbb{N}$. The range of sequence is the set of all distinct values of $a(n)$. The terms of a sequence are the values $a(1), a(2), …,a(n)$ which are usually denoted with subscripts as $a_1 , a_2 , ….a_n$ A sequence $a(n)$ is often denoted as ${a_n}$ . Limit of a Sequence Let ${a_n}$ be a sequence and let $L$ be a real number.

item.title: Antidifferentiation
item.description: Integration by Substitution Let $F$ and $g$ be diﬀerentiable functions, where the range of $g$ is an interval $I$ contained in the domain of $F$. Then $\displaystyle \int F’(g(x))g’(x) dx =F(g(x))+C $ If $u = g(x)$, then $du = g’(x)dx$ and $\displaystyle\int F’(g(x))g’(x) dx = \displaystyle\int F’(u)du = F(u)+C = F(g(x))+C$ Substitution With A Linear Function Consider $\int F ′ (ax + b) dx$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. Letting $u = ax + b$ gives $du = a · dx$, leading to the result

item.title: Integration
item.description: Antiderivatives and Indeﬁnite Integrals Let a function $f(x)$ be given. An antiderivative of $f(x)$ is a function $F(x)$ such that $F ′ (x) = f(x)$. The set of all antiderivatives of $f(x)$ is the indeﬁnite integral of $f$, denoted by $\displaystyle\int f(x) dx = F(x)+C$ Definite Integral The deﬁnite integral of $f$ on $[a, b]$ is the total signed area of $f$ on $[a, b]$, denoted $\displaystyle\int _a ^bf(x) dx$

item.title: Derivative
item.description: Definition Instantaneous rate of change at the point of the function. Presentation in Math,$f’(c)=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\dfrac {f(c+h)-f(c)} h$, the tangent line of $f$ at $c$ will go through $(c,f(c))$, the equation is $l(x)=f’(c)(x-c)+f(c)$. If we draw the derivatives of each point in the graph, we can get a new function called the derivative function, the equation is $f’(x)=\lim\limits_{h\to0}\dfrac {f(x+h)-f(x)} h$, It also writen as $f’(x)=y’=\dfrac {dy} {dx}=\dfrac {df} {dx}=\dfrac d {dx}(y)=\dfrac d {dx}(f)$

item.title: Limit
item.description: Introduction The graph of the function is contributed by many points in the same set. It is a straight line, curve or anything else. When the x-value of points approaching constant c, we’ll find the y-values of these points also approaching constant L. Definition $$ \lim_{x \to c}f(x)=L \iff \forall \ \epsilon > 0, \exists \ \delta > 0 \ s.t. |x-c| < \delta \rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\epsilon $$ Means that given any $ε > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all x in I, where x $\neq$ c, if $ | x − c | < \delta $, then $| f(x) − L | < ε$.
